title: Topic 375: Why Gaming Gets Blamed for Violence: Media Psychology
content:
# Topic 375: Why Gaming Gets Blamed for Violence: A Masterclass in Media Psychology and Moral Panics

## Abstract

This paper delves into the age-old tradition of blaming video games for real-world violence, a practice as reliable as a politician’s promise and as scientifically sound as a flat-earth convention. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the seductive allure of a simple scapegoat to the media’s insatiable appetite for a juicy narrative. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of moral panics, historical precedents, and the existing body of research, we demonstrate that the causal link between virtual mayhem and actual malice is about as robust as a chocolate teapot. This paper serves as a crucial reminder that while video games may be guilty of inducing carpal tunnel and a severe aversion to sunlight, they are innocent of the more heinous crimes they are so often accused of. The real culprit, we argue, is a far more complex and less entertaining cocktail of societal ills, which, unfortunately, can’t be defeated with a cheat code.

## Introduction

In the grand theater of public discourse, few villains have enjoyed as long and illustrious a career as the violent video game. For decades, this digital boogeyman has been trotted out after every societal ill, a convenient scapegoat for everything from teenage angst to mass violence. The narrative is as predictable as it is pervasive: a troubled youth, a penchant for pixelated carnage, and a tragic outcome. The media, with its characteristic subtlety, paints a lurid picture of a generation lost to the siren song of first-person shooters, their moral compasses shattered by a relentless barrage of virtual gore. Politicians, ever eager to demonstrate their commitment to protecting the children, join the chorus, their brows furrowed with a gravity usually reserved for discussions of tax reform and alien invasions.

But what if this narrative, so compelling in its simplicity, is nothing more than a well-crafted illusion? What if the link between video games and violence is less a scientific certainty and more a product of our collective psychological biases? This paper will argue that the demonization of video games is a classic case of a moral panic, a societal overreaction to a perceived threat that is amplified by the media and exploited by those with a political agenda. We will explore the psychological mechanisms that make us so susceptible to these panics, from our innate desire for simple explanations to our deep-seated fear of the new and unfamiliar. We will also examine the historical precedents for this phenomenon, from the comic book scare of the 1950s to the pinball panic of the 1970s, demonstrating that every generation has its own technological scapegoat. By the end of this paper, you will not only understand why video games get blamed for violence, but you will also be equipped with the critical thinking skills necessary to spot the next moral panic before it consumes us all. So, grab your tinfoil hats and your controllers, and let’s dive into the fascinating and often hilarious world of media psychology.

## Literature Review

The academic literature on video games and violence is a sprawling, chaotic battlefield, a digital Wild West where dueling studies clash in a cacophony of conflicting conclusions. It’s a place where one study will tell you that playing *Mortal Kombat* will turn your sweet little Timmy into a spine-ripping sociopath, while another will suggest that the only thing it’s likely to rip is a bag of Doritos. On one side, we have the alarmists, who, armed with correlational studies and a healthy dose of confirmation bias, declare that every headshot in *Call of Duty* is a step closer to a real-life rampage. Their studies often involve showing participants a violent image and then measuring their aggression by seeing how much hot sauce they’re willing to put on a stranger’s food. (Yes, really.) On the other side, we have the skeptics, who, with their meta-analyses and their insistence on pesky things like “causation,” argue that the link between virtual and real-world violence is about as strong as a wet paper bag. They point out that correlation does not equal causation, and that just because two things happen at the same time doesn’t mean one caused the other. After all, ice cream sales and shark attacks both increase in the summer, but we don’t blame Ben & Jerry’s for a rise in aquatic assaults. And in the middle, we have the American Psychological Association (APA), which, in a masterclass of diplomatic fence-sitting, has declared that while there is a “small, reliable association” between violent video games and aggressive behaviors like yelling and pushing, there is “insufficient scientific evidence to support a causal link” to more violent outcomes [1]. In other words, playing *Grand Theft Auto* might make you more likely to shout at your mom, but it’s not going to turn you into a real-life carjacker.

The concept of “moral panic,” first coined by sociologist Stanley Cohen, is particularly relevant here. A moral panic is a widespread fear, often irrational, that some evil person or thing threatens the well-being of society. These panics are typically fueled by the media and often lead to the passage of new laws or policies. The history of media is littered with moral panics, from the fear that comic books were turning children into juvenile delinquents in the 1950s [2] to the belief that Dungeons & Dragons was a gateway to Satanism in the 1980s. In each case, a new and poorly understood form of entertainment was blamed for a host of societal ills, with little to no evidence to support the claims. The video game panic is simply the latest iteration of this time-honored tradition, a digital update of an age-old story.

## Analysis

The enduring appeal of blaming video games for violence can be understood through the lens of several key psychological principles. First and foremost is the **fundamental attribution error**, our tendency to overestimate the importance of dispositional factors (like a person’s character or personality) and underestimate the importance of situational factors (like their environment or circumstances) when explaining their behavior. When a horrific act of violence occurs, it is far more comforting to believe that the perpetrator was simply a bad person, corrupted by a violent video game, than it is to confront the complex web of societal factors that may have contributed to their actions. It’s the psychological equivalent of blaming the car for a drunk driving accident. The video game becomes a convenient shorthand, a simple explanation for a complex and terrifying event. It’s a way of saying, “It’s not our fault. It’s the pixels.”

Another key factor is the **availability heuristic**, our tendency to overestimate the likelihood of events that are more easily recalled in memory. Thanks to the media’s relentless focus on the link between video games and violence, we are constantly bombarded with images and stories that reinforce this connection. Every time a school shooter is revealed to be a fan of *Doom*, the association is strengthened in our minds, making it seem far more common than it actually is. Meanwhile, the millions of well-adjusted, non-violent gamers who enjoy the same games are ignored, their existence a statistical inconvenience to the prevailing narrative.

Finally, we have the concept of **scapegoating**, the practice of singling out a person or group for unmerited blame and negative treatment. Throughout history, societies have used scapegoats to deflect blame and avoid confronting their own failings. In the case of video games, the industry has become a convenient scapegoat for a host of societal problems, from the decline of the nuclear family to the rise of mental illness. By blaming video games, we can avoid asking the difficult questions about our society and our values. It is a form of collective denial, a way of convincing ourselves that the problem is not with us, but with them – the gamers, the outsiders, the digital deviants.

## Real-World Examples

The history of video games is a veritable graveyard of moral panics, each one more absurd than the last. It’s a history so ridiculous, it makes the Salem witch trials look like a model of rational jurisprudence. The first shot was fired in 1976 with the release of *Death Race*, an arcade game in which players were tasked with running over gremlins. The media, in a fit of collective hysteria, declared that the game was teaching children to be reckless drivers and glorifying violence. The National Safety Council even got involved, presumably after a long and arduous day of warning people about the dangers of running with scissors. The game’s creator, Exidy, was inundated with angry letters from people who had never even seen the game, but were convinced it was a sign of the apocalypse. It was a classic case of what we in the business call “outrage porn.”

The 1990s saw the moral panic reach a fever pitch with the release of *Mortal Kombat* and *Night Trap*. The former, with its digitized sprites and over-the-top gore, was accused of turning children into bloodthirsty savages. The latter, a full-motion video game in which players had to protect a group of teenage girls from vampires, was accused of promoting sexual violence. The outcry was so great that it led to a series of congressional hearings, where politicians who had likely never held a controller in their lives pontificated on the dangers of this new and terrifying technology. The result was the creation of the Entertainment Software Rating Board (ESRB), a self-regulatory body that was designed to appease the moral crusaders and prevent government censorship.

But the most infamous example of a video game-related moral panic is, of course, the Columbine High School massacre in 1999. The two perpetrators were avid players of *Doom*, a fact that the media seized upon with a ghoulish glee. The game was blamed for everything from their nihilistic worldview to their tactical proficiency. The fact that they were also social outcasts who were relentlessly bullied was largely ignored, as was the fact that they had easy access to a small arsenal of weapons. The narrative was set: video games had created the monsters, and the only way to stop them was to ban the games.

## Discussion

The persistent and often hysterical focus on video games as a cause of violence has significant implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. By fixating on this one, relatively benign factor, we divert attention and resources away from the real causes of violence, such as poverty, mental illness, and the easy availability of firearms. This is not to say that video games have no effect on our behavior. Like any form of media, they can shape our attitudes and beliefs in subtle ways. But to suggest that they are a primary driver of violence is to engage in a dangerous form of magical thinking.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the moral panic surrounding video games is a fascinating case study in the power of narrative. The video game industry, once a niche hobby for a small group of enthusiasts, has become a multi-billion dollar behemoth, a cultural force that rivals Hollywood in its influence. This success has made it a target for those who are uncomfortable with its content and its reach. The moral panic is a way of pushing back against this cultural shift, of reasserting traditional values in the face of a rapidly changing world. It is a battle for the soul of our culture, and video games are simply the latest battlefield.

## Conclusion

In the end, the question of why video games get blamed for violence is not really about video games at all. It is about us. It is about our fears, our biases, and our desperate need for simple answers to complex questions. It is about our tendency to scapegoat the new and the unfamiliar, to demonize those who are different from us. And it is about our collective failure to address the real root causes of violence in our society.

Video games are not the enemy. They are a mirror, reflecting our own anxieties and our own desires. They are a canvas, upon which we project our own hopes and our own fears. And they are a scapegoat, a convenient target for our own failings. The next time you hear a politician or a pundit railing against the evils of violent video games, remember the lessons of history. Remember the comic books, the pinball machines, and the Dungeons & Dragons. And remember that the real monsters are not on the screen, but in the shadows of our own minds.
references:
American Psychological Association. (2020, March 3). *APA reaffirms position on violent video games and violent behavior* [Press release]. https://www.apa.org/news/press/releases/2020/03/violent-video-games-behavior
